title: The Domino Falls
bullets: In 376 the Goths massed on the Danube and begged to cross into the Roman Empire, fleeing a steppe people, the Huns, who had shattered the Greuthungi Gothic confederation at terrifying speed.

The Huns were steppe nomads whose composite bows and cavalry tactics left Roman infantry with nothing to grind down, and whose language was unclassifiable — certainly not Germanic, almost certainly not Indo-European.
At Adrianople in 378, Gothic cavalry destroyed the Eastern field army and killed Emperor Valens, the worst Roman military defeat in centuries and the first domino inside the empire.

Emperor Theodosius settled roughly 382 by admitting the Goths as foederati — armed settlers on Roman soil who kept their own kings and law in exchange for military service — establishing a precedent Rome would repeat to its ruin.

The foedus of 382 was fundamentally different from earlier barbarian settlements: the Goths arrived as an intact armed nation under its own command structure, not as a broken, divided people Rome had absorbed on its own terms.
Stilicho, the half-Vandal supreme commander of the West, spent the early 400s stripping Rhine frontier garrisons to fight crises closer to Italy, thinning the oldest fortified border in the empire.

On the last night of 406, with the Rhine thinned and multiple peoples pushed west by Hunnic pressure, Vandals, Suebi, and Alans crossed the frozen river and poured into Gaul, ending four and a half centuries of Roman frontier.

title: The Rhine Gives Way
bullets: Stilicho crushed Radagaisus's Gothic war-host near Florence in 405 but had to strip Rhine garrisons to do it, leaving the western frontier critically undermanned for the crossing that followed.

The Vandals and Suebi were Germanic peoples from east of the Rhine, but the Alans were an Iranian-speaking steppe people from the Caucasus region — the Rhine crossing was not one invasion but a continent's worth of displaced peoples clumping into a traveling alliance.

Roman troops in Britain, cut off and leaderless, proclaimed Constantine III emperor in 407 and stripped the island garrison to fight their civil war in Gaul, ending Roman military presence in Britain.

Around 410, the emperor Honorius told the British towns to see to their own defense, ending four centuries of Roman rule on the island without a dramatic withdrawal, just a request for help that was declined and never followed up.

In 408 Honorius had Stilicho executed on suspicion of barbarian sympathies, killing the West's last effective generalissimo at the exact moment the frontier was wide open — the chapter's bitterest irony.

The Rhine-crossing peoples ravaged Gaul for three years before being pushed across the Pyrenees into Hispania around 409, partly by the Visigoths doing exactly the federate job Rome had hired them for.

Within five years the West had lost a fortified Rhine border four centuries old, an entire province, its field army, and its only competent general — structural damage no subsequent recovery effort could fully undo.

title: Kingdoms Inside the Empire
bullets: The federate settlement system, which Roman governments used to manage peoples they could neither defeat nor expel, gradually transferred real power from the imperial court to barbarian kings governing intact armed nations inside Roman territory.

The first Burgundian kingdom on the Rhine was destroyed in the 430s by Aetius using Hunnic mercenaries, then resettled by treaty around Lake Geneva and the Rhône — demonstrating the pattern of settlement, destruction, and re-settlement that repeated across the West.

In 418 the Visigoths were formally settled in Aquitaine by Roman treaty with their capital at Toulouse, the prototype post-Roman successor state and the template for every formal province given to a barbarian king afterward.

The Alans, after heavy losses in Hispania, folded themselves into the Hasding Vandals rather than survive as a distinct people, a steppe Iranian people disappearing into a Germanic kingdom and surviving only as a title on a foreign king.

The Suebi settled the northwest corner of Hispania in 411 and built the most durable of the Rhine-crossing peoples' kingdoms, surviving until Visigothic absorption in 585 — outliving the Western Empire itself by more than a century.

The lesson of the 420s and 430s was that survival in the fifth century went not to the spectacular but to the boring: peoples who stopped migrating, found a corner no powerful neighbor wanted badly, and quietly became the local government.

By the 440s the Western government effectively controlled only Italy and a slice of southern Gaul, having already lost Britain, most of Spain, and the African breadbasket, a fortified remnant rather than an empire.

title: The Vandals
bullets: In 429 the Vandal king Geiseric ferried his entire people, roughly eighty thousand by the most-cited figure, across the Strait of Gibraltar into Roman North Africa, the single most valuable economic asset left in the Western Empire.

The target was not plunder but the annona, the centuries-old state grain artery that shipped African tax-in-kind to feed the city of Rome; controlling its African end was worth more strategically than sacking Rome itself.

Augustine of Hippo, the most influential Christian thinker of the ancient world, died during the Vandal siege of Hippo Regius in 430, an old man watching the Roman African world he had spent his life in being dismantled.

Geiseric took Carthage in 439, cutting off the Western Empire's largest revenue source and the machine that fed its capital, leaving the Western state fiscally hollow almost two decades before its formal end.

Geiseric did the thing no one expected a Germanic land people to do: he built a navy, making the Vandals the supreme maritime power of the western Mediterranean and raiding Sicily, Sardinia, and the Italian coast at will.

In 455 Geiseric sailed directly to Rome, plundered it for two weeks, and shipped imperial and religious treasure back to Carthage — the second sack of Rome in forty-five years, and this time the Western Empire was too weak even to retaliate.

Geiseric died in his bed in 477, undefeated, having taken a beaten land people that crossed the Rhine on foot in 406 and turned it into the dominant power of the western Mediterranean in a single lifetime.

title: Attila
bullets: Attila and his brother Bleda became joint kings of the Huns around 434; Attila murdered Bleda in 445 and ruled alone, concentrating all Hunnic power in a man whose entire strategy was systematic extraction of gold by terror.

Between 441 and 450 Attila devastated the Balkans and forced the Eastern Empire to pay a treaty tribute reaching roughly 6,300 pounds of gold annually — a protection racket run at the scale of an empire.

The Roman envoy Priscus visited Attila's court on the Hungarian plain in 449 and left the most vivid primary source of the era: timber halls, gold plate, Attila eating from a plain wooden platter while everyone around him used precious metal.

In 451 Attila invaded Gaul with a vast army, and Roman general Aetius assembled a coalition of Germanic kingdoms — Visigoths, Burgundians, Franks — to meet him at the Battle of the Catalaunian Plains, halting Attila in the largest battle of late antiquity.

The Catalaunian Plains was in its essence a coalition of Germanic kingdoms led by a Roman general against a Hunnic confederation that was itself mostly Germanic subject troops, the old Roman-versus-barbarian frame completely dissolved.

In 452 Attila invaded Italy and Pope Leo I led a delegation that met him on the Mincio; Attila withdrew, though disease, famine, and a Byzantine raid on his homeland almost certainly had as much to do with the retreat as the papal meeting.

Attila died suddenly in 453 and the Hunnic empire collapsed within a year; the Germanic subjects they had dominated since 375 destroyed the Hunnic army at the Battle of Nedao in 454, proving that a power built on one man is only as durable as the man.

title: The Fall of the West
bullets: Aetius, the last capable Western generalissimo, held the declining empire together through the 430s and 450s by managing barbarians with other barbarians, but in 454 Emperor Valentinian III murdered him at court with his own hand.

Between 455 and 476 nine emperors cycled through the Western throne in twenty-one years, almost all of them installed and discarded by Ricimer, a half-Visigothic Arian general who could not legally become emperor himself.

Emperor Majorian, 457–461, was the West's last serious recovery attempt, rebuilding a fleet to retake Vandal Africa, but his invasion fleet was burned at anchor by treachery and Ricimer had him executed five days after his return.

The Domain of Soissons, a rump Roman state in northern Gaul under Syagrius, kept collecting taxes and running Roman courts in Latin years after there was officially any Western Empire to belong to, surviving until the Franks destroyed it in 486.

In 476 the Germanic commander Odoacer deposed the boy-emperor Romulus Augustulus and sent the imperial regalia back to Constantinople, a deliberate statement that the West would have no more emperors of its own.

Julius Nepos, the Eastern-recognized legitimate Western emperor, was alive in Dalmatia until his murder in 480, making the Western Empire's legal extinction messier than the 476 date implies and the concept of a single 'fall' essentially a later periodization.

The fall was not a battle but a long structural dissolution — fiscal, military, and territorial — running from at least 406, in which the Western state lost the ability to tax, feed, and defend itself and was replaced from within by the kingdoms it had itself created.

title: The Successor Kingdoms
bullets: The decisive difference between the Franks and every other successor kingdom was religion: Clovis converted directly to Catholic Christianity around 496, while Visigoths, Vandals, Burgundians, and Ostrogoths all followed Arianism, the theology their Roman subjects regarded as heresy.

By converting to Catholicism rather than Arianism, Clovis turned the Catholic bishops — the most powerful surviving local administrators in the former Western Empire — from structural adversaries into crown partners and propagandists.

Clovis destroyed the last Roman domain in Gaul at the Battle of Soissons in 486, beat the Alemanni around 496, and then at the Battle of Vouillé in 507 drove the Visigoths out of Gaul entirely and confined them to Spain.

The Lex Salica, codified around 507–511, governed the Franks for centuries; one clause barring daughters from inheriting certain land was seized by lawyers a thousand years later and used to argue no woman could inherit the French crown, helping trigger the Hundred Years' War.

Theodoric the Great ruled Ostrogothic Italy for over thirty years from Ravenna, governing Romans through Roman administrators, keeping the Senate and law courts running, and employing Roman intellectuals like Boethius and Cassiodorus.

Boethius, imprisoned and awaiting execution by Theodoric on treason charges around 523, wrote the Consolation of Philosophy, which became one of the most copied books of the entire Middle Ages, read for a thousand years by people who found the argument convincing precisely because its author had no reason to be dishonest.

The Visigothic, Burgundian, and Ostrogothic law codes — Breviary of Alaric, Lex Salica, Gundobad's dual Roman and Burgundian codes — reveal that the successor kings saw themselves not as destroyers of Rome but as its executors, preserving Roman law for the Romans they ruled.

title: The Eastern Reconquest
bullets: Emperor Justinian came to power in 527 with an ambition most contemporaries thought unaffordable: the renovatio imperii, the military restoration of Roman rule across the former western provinces.

In 533 Belisarius landed in North Africa with a small professional army and destroyed the Vandal kingdom in months — two battles, Carthage taken almost unopposed, and the Vandal people drafted into the Eastern army and dissolved within a generation.

The Vandal collapse on land, despite a century of naval supremacy, proved how hollow even the most spectacular barbarian kingdom could be once a funded professional army landed behind the fleet and ignored it.

The Gothic War in Italy from 535 ground on for nearly twenty years because the Ostrogoths found capable leaders, above all King Totila, turning what was supposed to be a quick reconquest into a catastrophic back-and-forth that devastated the peninsula.

Rome changed hands multiple times during the Gothic War and at one point stood nearly empty; the city that survived five barbarian passages in fifty years was besieged or sacked five times in twenty years by armies both claiming to save it.

Narses finally ended the Gothic War around 552 and Justinian's Pragmatic Sanction of 554 formally re-established Roman law in Italy — but the reconquest had destroyed the last working fragment of the Roman world that the barbarians themselves had mostly preserved.

The barbarian kingdoms had every incentive to keep Roman Italy functioning because they were moving in; the most destructive event of the entire two-century collapse turned out to be the Roman Empire's own war to reverse it.

title: The End of Antiquity in the West
bullets: In 536 the sun dimmed for over a year, confirmed not just by contemporary writers across the Mediterranean but by matching tree-ring data and volcanic sulfate spikes in polar ice cores, beginning one of the coldest decades in two thousand years.

The crop failures of the 536 volcanic winter fell across the entire post-Roman world at exactly the moment that world was least able to feed itself, after a century of migrations, fiscal collapse, and twenty years of total war in Italy.

The Plague of Justinian arrived in 541, spread along the empire's surviving sea routes to North Africa, Italy, Gaul, Spain, and the British Isles, and is now confirmed as the first historically documented pandemic of Yersinia pestis — the same bacterium as the Black Death — by DNA recovered from sixth-century burials.

The plague broke the back of Justinian's reconquest: the Eastern Empire strong enough to retake Africa and Italy in the 530s could no longer raise the soldiers or the money to defend those gains by the 550s.

In 568, just fourteen years after Narses finished ruining and restoring Italy, the Lombards under King Alboin walked into an exhausted, plague-thinned peninsula almost unopposed, the last of the great Germanic migrations into the West.

The Lombards could not take everything: the Eastern Empire clung to fortified zones around Ravenna, Rome, and the south, producing the permanent patchwork of Lombard duchies and Byzantine enclaves that shaped Italian politics for centuries.

The popes of the late sixth century had quietly become the local state, organizing the grain supply, paying off Lombard war-bands, running charitable networks, and governing Rome's hinterland because no one else could — the spiritual office filling the vacuum where imperial administration had been.

The migration period ended not with a decisive battle but with a volcanic winter, a plague, and a continent that had been hollowed out by its own attempts at self-preservation, handing forward the Catholic Frankish kingdom and the Roman Church as the two institutions capable of building what came next.
